A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model projects the cash adidas is expected to generate in the future and then discounts those cash flows back to a single value in today’s money. That figure is then compared with the current share price.

For adidas, the last twelve months free cash flow is reported at €211.64m. data.title: MilDef Group (OM:MILDEF) TTM Return To Profit Tests Bullish Turnaround Narrative
data.description:
MilDef Group (OM:MILDEF) has backed up its recent turnaround with Q4 2025 numbers that show total revenue of SEK 782.7 million and basic EPS of SEK 1.98, with trailing twelve month figures at SEK 2.0 billion of revenue and SEK 3.16 of EPS. The company has seen revenue move from SEK 417.5 million in Q4 2024 to SEK 782.7 million in Q4 2025, while net income shifted from a loss of SEK 249.4 million to a profit of SEK 93.4 million. This frames a cleaner earnings base for you to judge the latest release. Overall, the story here is about margins rebuilding on a higher revenue run rate. This sets up the rest of the year as a test of how durable this profitability really is.

See our full analysis for MilDef Group.

With the headline numbers on the table, the next step is to see how they line up against the widely followed narratives around MilDef Group's growth potential, risk profile, and earnings quality.

See what the community is saying about MilDef Group

Advertisement

TTM profit swings from loss of SEK 220.3 million to profit of SEK 148 million

  • On a trailing twelve month basis, net income moved from a loss of SEK 220.3 million in Q4 2024 to a profit of SEK 148 million in Q4 2025, with TTM revenue rising from SEK 1,200.9 million to SEK 2,045.2 million over the same points in time.
  • What stands out for the bearish narrative is that this return to profit sits alongside a five year average earnings decline of 40.2% a year, so:
    • bears highlight that the recent SEK 148 million profit arrives after several years where headline earnings were negative. This is why they question how steady this new earnings base really is, and
    • the Q4 2025 quarterly net income of SEK 93.4 million already accounts for a large share of the TTM SEK 148 million figure. Critics see this as a concentration risk if future quarters are lumpier.
Skeptical investors point to these sharp swings in profit as a reason to treat long term earnings targets cautiously, especially when a big part of the recent turnaround is packed into a single quarter, then refer back to the full bearish case in 🐻 MilDef Group Bear Case.

Revenue run rate rises from SEK 249.9 million to SEK 782.7 million per quarter

  • Looking at the last six reported quarters, quarterly revenue stepped from SEK 249.9 million in Q3 2024 to SEK 417.5 million in Q4 2024 and then to SEK 339.5 million, SEK 383.3 million, SEK 539.7 million and finally SEK 782.7 million in Q4 2025, while basic EPS moved from a loss of SEK 5.83 in Q4 2024 to a profit of SEK 1.98 in Q4 2025.
  • Consensus narrative links this higher revenue base to MilDef winning more complex system and integration work, and the recent numbers give some backing and some pushback to that, because:
    • the TTM revenue line at SEK 2,045.2 million and the order backlog references in the narrative are consistent with the idea that larger turnkey contracts are flowing into recognized sales, and
    • at the same time, the quarterly pattern from SEK 339.5 million up to SEK 782.7 million shows that revenue can still be quite lumpy from one period to the next. This keeps the risk of bumpy cash generation that consensus says could unsettle investors if project timing shifts.

DCF fair value of SEK 294.42 vs price of SEK 178.15 with a 56.7x P/E tag

  • MilDef is trading at SEK 178.15 per share against a stated DCF fair value of SEK 294.42 and an analyst price target of SEK 208.40, while the trailing P/E of 56.7x sits above both the peer average of 48.5x and the European Aerospace & Defense industry average of 36.1x.
  • Bullish investors argue that forecast earnings growth of 29.4% a year and revenue growth of 14.5% a year help justify that premium multiple, and the data cut both ways here, because:
    • the move from a TTM loss of SEK 220.3 million in Q4 2024 to a TTM profit of SEK 148 million in Q4 2025 provides the earnings base that makes a P/E ratio usable again. This supports the idea that the business has already crossed an important profitability hurdle, and
    • however, the fact that the current P/E is still higher than peer and industry levels, even with that new earnings base, means the price already reflects a degree of optimism about those 29.4% and 14.5% growth forecasts actually showing up in future reported numbers.

data.title: Earnings Release: Here's Why Analysts Cut Their HAKI Safety AB (publ) (STO:HAKI B) Price Target To kr29.00
data.description:
HAKI Safety AB (publ) (STO:HAKI B) came out with its first-quarter results last week, and we wanted to see how the business is performing and what industry forecasts think of the company following this report. It was a workmanlike result, with revenues of kr286m coming in 2.5% ahead of expectations, and statutory earnings per share of kr1.22, in line with analyst appraisals. The analyst typically update their forecasts at each earnings report, and we can judge from their estimates whether their view of the company has changed or if there are any new concerns to be aware of. With this in mind, we've gathered the latest statutory forecasts to see what the analyst is expecting for next year.

This technology could replace computers: discover the 20 stocks are working to make quantum computing a reality.

Taking into account the latest results, the consensus forecast from HAKI Safety's solitary analyst is for revenues of kr1.24b in 2026. This reflects a modest 3.5% improvement in revenue compared to the last 12 months. Statutory per-share earnings are expected to be kr1.72, roughly flat on the last 12 months. Before this earnings report, the analyst had been forecasting revenues of kr1.24b and earnings per share (EPS) of kr2.07 in 2026. The analyst seem to have become more bearish following the latest results. While there were no changes to revenue forecasts, there was a substantial drop in EPS estimates.

See our latest analysis for HAKI Safety

It might be a surprise to learn that the consensus price target fell 9.4% to kr29.00, with the analyst clearly linking lower forecast earnings to the performance of the stock price.

Looking at the bigger picture now, one of the ways we can make sense of these forecasts is to see how they measure up against both past performance and industry growth estimates. It's pretty clear that there is an expectation that HAKI Safety's revenue growth will slow down substantially, with revenues to the end of 2026 expected to display 4.7% growth on an annualised basis. This is compared to a historical growth rate of 7.2% over the past five years. Compare this against other companies (with analyst forecasts) in the industry, which are in aggregate expected to see revenue growth of 6.6% annually. So it's pretty clear that, while revenue growth is expected to slow down, the wider industry is also expected to grow faster than HAKI Safety.

The Bottom Line

The biggest concern is that the analyst reduced their earnings per share estimates, suggesting business headwinds could lay ahead for HAKI Safety. Fortunately, the analyst also reconfirmed their revenue estimates, suggesting that it's tracking in line with expectations. Although our data does suggest that HAKI Safety's revenue is expected to perform worse than the wider industry. The consensus price target fell measurably, with the analyst seemingly not reassured by the latest results, leading to a lower estimate of HAKI Safety's future valuation.

Keeping that in mind, we still think that the longer term trajectory of the business is much more important for investors to consider. We have analyst estimates for HAKI Safety going out as far as 2028, and you can see them free on our platform here.

data.title: It Might Not Be A Great Idea To Buy Söder Sportfiske AB (STO:SODER) For Its Next Dividend
data.description:
It looks like Söder Sportfiske AB (STO:SODER) is about to go ex-dividend in the next 4 days. The ex-dividend date generally occurs two days before the record date, which is the day on which shareholders need to be on the company's books in order to receive a dividend. It is important to be aware of the ex-dividend date because any trade on the stock needs to have been settled on or before the record date. Meaning, you will need to purchase Söder Sportfiske's shares before the 29th of April to receive the dividend, which will be paid on the 6th of May.

The company's upcoming dividend is kr00.80 a share, following on from the last 12 months, when the company distributed a total of kr0.80 per share to shareholders. Looking at the last 12 months of distributions, Söder Sportfiske has a trailing yield of approximately 4.3% on its current stock price of kr018.70. We love seeing companies pay a dividend, but it's also important to be sure that laying the golden eggs isn't going to kill our golden goose! That's why we should always check whether the dividend payments appear sustainable, and if the company is growing.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Dividends are typically paid out of company income, so if a company pays out more than it earned, its dividend is usually at a higher risk of being cut. It paid out 87% of its earnings as dividends last year, which is not unreasonable, but limits reinvestment in the business and leaves the dividend vulnerable to a business downturn. We'd be worried about the risk of a drop in earnings. Yet cash flows are even more important than profits for assessing a dividend, so we need to see if the company generated enough cash to pay its distribution. It paid out more than half (58%) of its free cash flow in the past year, which is within an average range for most companies.

It's positive to see that Söder Sportfiske's dividend is covered by both profits and cash flow, since this is generally a sign that the dividend is sustainable, and a lower payout ratio usually suggests a greater margin of safety before the dividend gets cut.

Check out our latest analysis for Söder Sportfiske

Click here to see how much of its profit Söder Sportfiske paid out over the last 12 months.

Advertisement

Have Earnings And Dividends Been Growing?

When earnings decline, dividend companies become much harder to analyse and own safely. Investors love dividends, so if earnings fall and the dividend is reduced, expect a stock to be sold off heavily at the same time. With that in mind, we're discomforted by Söder Sportfiske's 14% per annum decline in earnings in the past five years. Such a sharp decline casts doubt on the future sustainability of the dividend.

The main way most investors will assess a company's dividend prospects is by checking the historical rate of dividend growth. In the past two years, Söder Sportfiske has increased its dividend at approximately 15% a year on average. The only way to pay higher dividends when earnings are shrinking is either to pay out a larger percentage of profits, spend cash from the balance sheet, or borrow the money. Söder Sportfiske is already paying out 87% of its profits, and with shrinking earnings we think it's unlikely that this dividend will grow quickly in the future.

Final Takeaway

Has Söder Sportfiske got what it takes to maintain its dividend payments? While earnings per share are shrinking, it's encouraging to see that at least Söder Sportfiske's dividend appears sustainable, with earnings and cashflow payout ratios that are within reasonable bounds. Bottom line: Söder Sportfiske has some unfortunate characteristics that we think could lead to sub-optimal outcomes for dividend investors.

data.description:

data.title: We Think That There Are Issues Underlying Robertet's (EPA:RBT) Earnings
data.description:
Robertet SA's (EPA:RBT) robust earnings report didn't manage to move the market for its stock. Our analysis suggests that this might be because shareholders have noticed some concerning underlying factors.

AI is about to change healthcare. These 20 stocks are working on everything from early diagnostics to drug discovery. The best part - they are all under $10bn in marketcap - there is still time to get in early.

One essential aspect of assessing earnings quality is to look at how much a company is diluting shareholders. As it happens, Robertet issued 7.3% more new shares over the last year. As a result, its net income is now split between a greater number of shares. To celebrate net income while ignoring dilution is like rejoicing because you have a single slice of a larger pizza, but ignoring the fact that the pizza is now cut into many more slices. You can see a chart of Robertet's EPS by clicking here.

Advertisement

A Look At The Impact Of Robertet's Dilution On Its Earnings Per Share (EPS)

Robertet has improved its profit over the last three years, with an annualized gain of 36% in that time. And in the last year the company managed to bump profit up by 15%. On the other hand, earnings per share are only up 9.8% in that time. And so, you can see quite clearly that dilution is influencing shareholder earnings.

Changes in the share price do tend to reflect changes in earnings per share, in the long run. So it will certainly be a positive for shareholders if Robertet can grow EPS persistently. But on the other hand, we'd be far less excited to learn profit (but not EPS) was improving. For the ordinary retail shareholder, EPS is a great measure to check your hypothetical "share" of the company's profit.

That might leave you wondering what analysts are forecasting in terms of future profitability. Luckily, you can click here to see an interactive graph depicting future profitability, based on their estimates.

Our Take On Robertet's Profit Performance

Robertet shareholders should keep in mind how many new shares it is issuing, because, dilution clearly has the power to severely impact shareholder returns. Because of this, we think that it may be that Robertet's statutory profits are better than its underlying earnings power. But at least holders can take some solace from the 30% per annum growth in EPS for the last three. Of course, we've only just scratched the surface when it comes to analysing its earnings; one could also consider margins, forecast growth, and return on investment, among other factors. Ultimately, this article has formed an opinion based on historical data. However, it can also be great to think about what analysts are forecasting for the future. Luckily, you can check out what analysts are forecasting by clicking here.

Today we've zoomed in on a single data point to better understand the nature of Robertet's profit. But there are plenty of other ways to inform your opinion of a company. Some people consider a high return on equity to be a good sign of a quality business. data.title: IBM (IBM) Margin Surge Reinforces Bullish Narratives Despite Slower Forecast Growth
data.description:
International Business Machines (IBM) opened 2026 on the back of a robust finish to 2025, with Q4 revenue of US$19.7b and basic EPS of US$5.96 feeding into trailing 12 month revenue of US$67.5b and EPS of US$11.34, alongside a 75.7% year over year earnings increase. Over the last year, revenue has moved from US$62.8b on a trailing basis to US$67.5b. Quarterly EPS has ranged from US$1.14 in Q1 2025 to US$5.96 in Q4 2025 as net profit margin improved from 9.6% to 15.7%. With that backdrop of higher profitability and earnings growth, the latest results set the stage for investors to weigh sustained margin strength against more moderate forecast growth.

See our full analysis for International Business Machines.

With the headline numbers in place, the next step is to see how this earnings profile lines up with the key narratives investors follow, from growth expectations to the quality and durability of those higher margins.

See what the community is saying about International Business Machines

Advertisement

Margins punch above revenue growth

  • Trailing 12 month net profit margin sits at 15.7% on US$67.5b of revenue and US$10.6b of net income, compared with a 9.6% margin a year earlier on US$62.8b of revenue.
  • What stands out for the bullish view is that trailing earnings grew 75.7% year over year against a 24.4% five year average growth rate. Bulls also expect margins to reach 17.1% in coming years, so the current 15.7% level already leans toward that thesis but sits alongside forecasts for earnings growth of about 8.15% a year, which is more modest than the recent history.
    • Supporters of the bullish story point to this higher margin base on US$19.7b of Q4 revenue and US$5.96 of EPS as evidence that higher value hybrid cloud and software are gaining weight in the mix.
    • At the same time, the forecast 4.8% annual revenue growth is below the US market averages cited, which means bulls rely heavily on sustained margin strength rather than fast top line expansion.

Bulls argue that IBM's shift toward higher margin software and AI could make this 15.7% profitability level more durable, especially if hybrid cloud and automation keep scaling across its large client base. It is worth seeing how that case is built out in more depth through the 🐂 International Business Machines Bull Case

Valuation sits between P/E and DCF signals

  • The shares trade at US$231.08 with a trailing P/E of 20.5x, above the 14.2x peer average but in line with the US IT industry at 20.5x. A DCF fair value of US$319.89 indicates a sizeable gap between the current price and that modelled estimate.
  • Bears focus on the risk that slower forecast growth of about 8.15% earnings and 4.8% revenue annually may not justify a P/E at the industry level, even with that DCF fair value sitting above the share price and a single analyst price target reference of about US$284.18.
    • Critics highlight that both revenue and earnings growth forecasts sit below the broader US market rates in the dataset, which can limit how much multiple expansion investors are willing to support.
    • On the other hand, the 2.91% dividend yield and the 75.7% trailing earnings growth rate are the kinds of signals that often help support valuation, so bears lean on the growth slowdown to argue that some of this strength may already be reflected in the current P/E.

Skeptics who worry that IBM's slower forecast growth may not support its current P/E often point to these mixed valuation signals alongside balance sheet concerns, and that cautious angle is set out in more detail in the 🐻 International Business Machines Bear Case

Debt and dividend shape the risk profile

  • The company pairs a 2.91% dividend yield with what is described as a high debt level, so investors are effectively trading income against balance sheet leverage in addition to the core earnings story.
  • Consensus narrative notes that this combination of leverage and dividend income is being weighed against forecast margin expansion, where analysts model profit margins rising from around 9.1% in their base case to 14.1% in the coming years, compared with the current 15.7% figure in the trailing data.
    • For income focused holders, the key tension is that elevated debt can limit flexibility for future buybacks or higher dividends if cash flow growth slows from the recent 75.7% earnings growth pace toward the forecast 8.15% per year.
    • For more growth oriented investors, the margin expansion embedded in forecasts needs to be squared with the fact that both revenue and earnings growth are expected to sit below the US market averages cited in the dataset, which can cap enthusiasm if debt reduction also becomes a priority.

Next Steps

data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Super Micro Computer (SMCI) After Recent Price Swings And Governance Concerns
data.description:

The share price recently closed at US$26.75, with returns of 20.3% over the last 30 days, even though the stock shows a 5.8% decline over 7 days and a 25.3% decline over 1 year, alongside a very large 3 year return and a 602.1% return over 5 years.

Approach 1: Super Micro Computer Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company is worth today by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to a single present value figure.

For Super Micro Computer, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on current last twelve month Free Cash Flow of about $393.95 million. Analysts provide explicit Free Cash Flow estimates for the earlier years, and Simply Wall St extrapolates further out to build a full 10 year path. Under this framework, projected Free Cash Flow for 2035 is about $2.62b, expressed in today's dollars as just over $1.09b after discounting.

Bringing all of those discounted cash flows together, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of US$43.58 per share. Versus the recent share price of US$26.75, this implies the stock is 38.6% undervalued according to this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Super Micro Computer Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful way to check how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It tends to be a core driver of long term shareholder returns. A higher or lower P/E often reflects what the market expects for future growth and how much risk investors see in those earnings.

Super Micro Computer currently trades on a P/E of 18.40x. That sits below the broader Tech industry average P/E of 23.36x and well below the peer group average of 44.74x. On the surface, that points to a lower earnings multiple than many comparable companies.
